title: The AI experiment is over: Australian leaders now have to move
full_text:
IN PARTNERSHIP WITH Deloitte The trial-and-error phase of AI is finished and it’s time to get serious. Australian organisations can’t keep dragging their feet, they must make clear-cut decisions about which AI programs will be scaled up, and which will be left behind.
That was one key takeaway from Deloitte CONNECT 2026, Deloitte’s forum for senior leaders across Australia to come together and discuss what is changing in business and technology.
During discussions on scaling enterprise AI, leaders made it clear that decisions on which AI initiatives should move beyond pilot mode and be scaled across organisations must be made within the next year.
The pressure is real because Australia is behind the curve. According to the Deloitte State of AI in the Enterprise Report, only 65 per cent of Australian respondents intend to lift AI investment next year, versus 84 per cent globally. Just 12 per cent of Australian leaders say generative AI is already transforming their business or industry, compared with 25 per cent globally.
One reason Australia is falling behind is organisational, not technical. Many organisations say culture is hugely important for AI adoption, but far fewer are actively addressing it. Most have not redesigned work and roles, and few believe they manage change effectively.
The temptation is often to treat AI as just another line item in the technology budget. But with agentic AI, consumption and operating cost can move sharply as usage patterns shift. That makes software and infrastructure decisions board-level issues.
However, the cost of scaling AI cannot be discounted. A large-scale AI assistant is made up of many parts, including different models, data transfers, security and monitoring. Collectively, these parts change unit economics and can quickly drive up costs, especially if leadership leaves AI usage unchecked by not setting clear standards for what is worth scaling.
SAP ANZ President and Managing Director Angela Colantuono said the real challenge is not building more tools but rethinking how work gets done: “The real value of AI isn’t in creating more technology layers, it’s in embedding intelligence directly into the way people already work. By making it easier to access and act on data within SAP systems, we can drive meaningful business outcomes. It’s this shift, from stand-alone tools to AI embedded in core processes, that will ultimately unlock scale and value.”
Costs jump when complexity compounds At scale, the bill is shaped by factors like algorithmic intensity, and leaders are seeing tougher standards for measurable return. The response is not to slow down, but to get specific about where the biggest value pools are, which workloads should scale, and what guardrails keep the spend from outrunning value.
Cathy Conroy, RVP Partners & Alliances ANZ at Snowflake, emphasises the necessity of implementing guardrails as systems expand: “The model wants to please you. That’s how models are built. That’s why at this data layer you have to have governance, access control and guardrails, or the models will continue to try to answer the question whether they should or not.” Without that discipline, risks and costs grow quickly.
Factory thinking turns pilots into scale Factory thinking is about building a repeatable way of delivering AI, so once it works, it can be scaled across an organisation without starting from scratch each time. It combines consistent processes, clear guardrails and a strong test-and-learn approach to turn early wins into reliable, repeatable outcomes.
As Sherif Mansour, Head of AI and Product Craft at Atlassian, explains, scaling AI is ultimately an organisational learning challenge: “The most important thing at this stage is maximising your learning loops, because what you’re trying to do in this world is going to change in six months and in 12 months.” In other words, the ability to learn quickly and adapt matters just as much as the technology itself.
Frank Fillmann, EVP & General Manager at Salesforce Australia and New Zealand, put it this way: “If you’re tinkering with curiosity but not in a way that can graduate safely with risk and compliance, you get great ideas and no one knows what to do with them. When you find gold, you need to scale it across the organisation.” Software and infrastructure decisions are crucial, as cost and risk depend on how things are designed and used. Making careful choices supports safe and scalable growth. It’s just as important to invest in organisational change, so AI becomes part of daily business, delivering consistent value rather than just being an experiment.
Australian organisations have strong AI ambitions but need to act. Leaders must make decisions that manage uncertainty and risk — waiting is riskier. By focusing on value, factory thinking and disciplined economics, businesses can gain an advantage. Those who delay may miss out as the market moves ahead. Bec McConnochie is Industry, Sectors & Alliances Lead Partner at Deloitte Australia. This article is part three of a three-part series based on Deloitte CONNECT.
